commitments.text: In Case1, the judge MUST NOT frame the innocent person, even to save five hostages
In Case2, the driver MAY steer onto the side track to kill one workman instead of five
In Case3, the bystander MAY throw the switch to divert the trolley to kill one workman instead of five

In Case4, the bystander MAY push the heavy man onto the track ONLY IF: (a) it is the absolute last resort, (b) there is certainty of saving more lives, and (c) no alternative exists to prevent catastrophic harm
In Case5, the bystander MUST NOT take any action, as both switch options lead to morally unacceptable outcomes

theory.text: When an action would reduce overall harm, by diverting between morally unacceptable outcomes, a moral agent is permitted to choose the option that minimizes harm to innocent persons.

A moral agent must not actively and directly cause harm to an innocent person, even if doing so would prevent greater harm; however, a moral agent may passively redirect existing harm to minimize overall loss of innocent life.

In extreme scenarios where an individual's involuntary sacrifice would definitively prevent a significantly greater loss of innocent life, a moral agent may consider using that individual as a means to prevent catastrophic harm, but only under strict conditions of certainty and last resort.

initial_commitments.text: In Case1, the judge MUST NOT frame the innocent person, even to save five hostages
In Case2, the driver MAY steer onto the side track to kill one workman instead of five
In Case3, the bystander MAY throw the switch to divert the trolley to kill one workman instead of five
In Case4, the bystander MUST NOT push the heavy man onto the track to stop the trolley
In Case5, the bystander MUST NOT take any action, as both switch options lead to morally unacceptable outcomes

systematicity.rationale: An empty theory has no principles, no structure, and no explanatory power. It provides no systematic account of anything, resulting in the lowest possible systematicity score.

In Case4, the bystander MAY push the heavy man onto the track ONLY IF: (a) it is the absolute last resort, (b) there is certainty of saving more lives, and (c) no alternative exists to prevent catastrophic harm
In Case5, the bystander MUST NOT take any action, as both switch options lead to morally unacceptable outcomes

theory.text: When an action would reduce overall harm, by diverting between morally unacceptable outcomes, a moral agent is permitted to choose the option that minimizes harm to innocent persons.

A moral agent must not actively and directly cause harm to an innocent person, even if doing so would prevent greater harm; however, a moral agent may passively redirect existing harm to minimize overall loss of innocent life.

In extreme scenarios where an individual's involuntary sacrifice would definitively prevent a significantly greater loss of innocent life, a moral agent may consider using that individual as a means to prevent catastrophic harm, but only under strict conditions of certainty and last resort.

The moral permissibility of an involuntary sacrifice is contingent upon: (a) absolute certainty of preventing greater harm, (b) the absence of any alternative interventions, (c) the individual not being deliberately targeted due to personal characteristics, and (d) a proportional relationship between the potential loss of life.

account.rationale: IC1=1.0: P2 directly supports not framing an innocent person, even for a seemingly greater good.  |  IC2=1.0: P1 supports diverting harm to minimize overall loss of innocent life by redirecting an existing threat.  |  IC3=1.0: P1 and P2 together support passively redirecting existing harm to minimize loss of life.  |  IC4=1.0: P3 precisely matches the conditions for using an individual to prevent catastrophic harm, emphasizing last resort, certainty, and minimizing overall harm.  |  IC5=1.0: P2 implies avoiding direct harm to innocent persons when both outcomes are morally unacceptable.

systematicity.rationale: Theory T demonstrates high systematicity through three interconnected principles that establish a coherent framework for moral decision-making under harm reduction scenarios. The principles progressively refine a core ethical logic around minimizing harm to innocent persons, with P1, P2, and P3 building a nuanced, increasingly specific set of guidelines that maintain internal consistency while addressing complex moral dilemmas.

The principles are abstract and generalizable, covering scenarios of harm mitigation while maintaining consistent internal logic about the moral constraints on sacrificing innocent life.
